text: Hello everyone, this is Roxanne Liu from Oakland, California. I'm back with another challenging inguinal hernia repair. This is a video demonstrating a robotic left inguinal hernia repair and a sigmoid colon is stuck in there and part of the sigmoid colon is part of the hernia sac, makes up the wall of the hernia sac, hence it's a colon slider. The patient has a scrotal hernia where you could feel stool concretions within the colon in the hernia sac. These concretions are about the size of a golf ball. The hernia definitely is not reducible on physical exam, even with the patient relaxed. After induction of anesthesia, I was hoping that it would be reducible, but it still wasn't.

The surgeon attempts to reduce the hernia from within the abdomen with assistance from the bedside team. Four ports are utilized, including one in the right lower quadrant. The surgeon begins creating a high peritoneal flap to ensure adequate working space and potential placement of a large mesh.

The surgeon navigates the thin peritoneum and enters the retro-rectus space to prevent the flap from tearing. The posterior rectus sheath is cut to connect the retro-rectus space with the lateral visceral preperitoneal space. A wide flap is created, and the left medial umbilical ligament is divided. Care is taken to avoid cutting the linea alba to prevent an incisional hernia.

The surgeon navigates the lower abdomen where the posterior rectus sheath becomes attenuated, crossing the midline into the contralateral retrorectus space. Utilizing the fourth robotic arm for flap retraction, the surgeon employs both hands for dissection. The transverse salus sling and surrounding tissue outside the hernia sac are divided. The surgeon identifies the plane between the testicular vessels and peritoneum, mindful of the slider hernia where the bowel wall may form the sac. Cauterization is performed on the superficial side of the tissue to avoid the bowel, ensuring safe dissection.

The surgeon navigates around firm connective tissue, likely fibrotic due to the colon sliding in and out of the internal ring. They carefully free the hernia, using the fourth arm to retract and expose the transverse alias sling. The surgeon identifies and preserves the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve and a visible femoral branch, while exploring for a plane between the hernia contents and surrounding structures. The testicular vessels remain adhered to the sac.

The surgeon identifies the colon forming part of the hernia wall and attempts to find the plane between the testicular vessels and the hernia sac. Fatty tissue is being separated from the hernia contents, likely revealing the testicular vessels. The surgeon uses a Cartier instrument to retract the bowel, ensuring not to apply excessive force. The dissection continues broadly, with attention to the inferior epigastric vessels and the vas deferens, which is more posterior than expected. The surgeon uses cautery to divide fibrous tissue anteriorly.

The surgeon is dissecting the hernia sac in the indirect space, encountering significant scar tissue. The medial structures are divided, allowing further dissection of the hernia sac. The surgeon carefully removes peripheral attachments from the sac, revealing a white edge indicative of the direct inguinal hernia plane. Despite not yet locating the testicular vessels, the dissection progresses smoothly with minimal bleeding.

The surgeon maintains hemostasis while attempting to reduce the colon from the hernia sac. Stool concretions are visible as they are pulled through the internal opening. The surgeon identifies and separates the testicular vessels from the hernia sac, ensuring minimal bleeding. Fat is cauterized off the anterior side of the hernia sac, while the posterior side remains attached to the colon mesentery.

The surgeon identifies a fibrotic band preventing the hernia from reducing and uses cautery near the bowel, carefully lifting tissue away to avoid injury. The plane between the testicular vessels and the colon is visible. The surgeon grasps the testicular vessels to facilitate dissection, exploring the fibrotic tissue and cauterizing as needed. The hernia sac is reduced, and the surgeon continues to find the plane between the testicular vessels and the hernia contents.

The surgeon identifies and dissects the fatty tissue from the hernia sac, aiming to expose the white surface of the sac. The testicular vessels have already been separated from the sac, allowing for efficient removal of the fatty tissue. The surgeon finds a new plane between the testicular vessels and the remaining colon, and considers transecting the sac if necessary, as most of the bowel has been reduced.

The surgeon separates the testicular vessels from the hernia sac, identifying the leading edge of the sac amidst fibrotic tissue. Using a third instrument through a fourth port for retraction, the surgeon carefully dissects around enlarged vessels, ensuring they are bipolar before division. The vas deferens is located by hugging the sac and dissecting layer by layer, avoiding injury. The surgeon lifts away from critical structures like the iliac vein, navigating through scar tissue and challenging areas near the bladder.

The surgeon works to obtain the critical view of the myopectineal orifice, crossing midline and dissecting two centimeters deep into the retropubic space. A large piece of mesh, measuring seventeen by twelve centimeters, is prepared for use due to the size of the inguinal hernia. Hemostasis is maintained to avoid injury to the bladder and minimize bleeding. The surgeon divides a vessel obstructing the medial aspect of the space and ensures the mesh sits flush against the superior pubic rami by skeletonizing the testicular vessels and vas deferens. Care is taken to avoid injuring the iliac vein while dissecting the areolar plane between bladder fat and iliac vein fat.

The surgeon completes the dissection in the space of Bogros, ensuring no injury to the lateral femoral cutaneous and femoral branch nerves, which are protected by a shiny layer of tissue. The mesh is positioned medially with the long line parallel to the allopubic tract and the vertical short line near the vas deferens. The mesh is tucked into the space of Retzius with good posterior lateral coverage to prevent recurrence. A three-oh absorbable suture is placed superior laterally, and another stitch is added in Cooper's ligament for stability.

The surgeon deploys the mesh, ensuring it covers the surgical area effectively. A third stitch is placed to secure the mesh, followed by the closure of the peritoneal flap. The large hernia sac is left intact due to its wide mouth and colon involvement, with large bites taken to reduce redundancy. The Cartier instrument assists in holding the sac up for efficient closure.

The surgeon desufflates the abdomen to four millimeters mercury to evacuate CO₂ during flap closure, ensuring the peritoneum pushes up against the mesh. The mesh is tucked in nicely, with the posterior edge checked for clamshelling. The nurse increases insufflation to fifteen millimeters mercury, and a small hole in the peritoneum is repaired. The peritoneum is seen pushed up against the hernia mesh, showing the lattice imprint, with the mesh tucked in well and slightly curled laterally.
